title: Matthew 7
subtitle: Judge Not; Ask, Seek, Knock; The Two Ways; The Two Builders

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Sermon on the Mount

sections.header: Verses 1–5 — Do Not Judge; the Speck and the Plank

heb.paragraph: 'Do not judge' uses krinō in its censorious sense — harsh, final condemnation. The passage does not forbid moral discernment (v.6 requires it). It forbids the presumption of occupying God's bench, pronouncing verdicts on others while ignoring one's own defects.

The absurd image — a plank in your eye while criticising a speck in another's — is deliberate hyperbole designed to provoke laughter and shame simultaneously. Jesus uses comedy to expose the universal human tendency toward self-exempting moral criticism.

hist.context: The opening of ch.7 transitions from the Sermon's teaching on piety (ch.6) to its teaching on community relationships and the conditions of genuine discipleship. The four units (judging, pearls/pigs, ask/seek/knock, golden rule) are united by the theme of discernment: knowing when to correct and when to hold back, knowing how to pray, knowing how to treat others as you would be treated.

refs.note: Love your neighbour as yourself — the golden rule (v.12) is its positive expression. "Do to others what you would have them do to you" activates love as the governing principle of all Torah.
All the Law and the Prophets hang on the double love command — the same claim made of the golden rule in 7:12. The Sermon and the controversy section both point to the same summary.

notes.note: Calvin: The prohibition targets the censorious spirit that appoints itself judge over all others while exempting itself. It does not forbid church discipline or discernment of false teaching. The test: is the judgment accompanied by the same standard applied to oneself?

Calvin on the two ways: The narrow gate is the gospel's entrance -- it requires the renunciation of all human righteousness and total dependence on Christ. The wide gate admits all -- self-righteous religion, moral achievement, human philosophy -- everything except the humble acknowledgment of need.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'do not judge' discusses the judicial background: krino was used for formal legal judgment. The prohibition is against setting oneself up as final arbiter of another's standing before God. Practical discernment (v.6) and church discipline (18:15-20) are not prohibited.

The NET note on 'how much more' (poso mallon) identifies the classical a fortiori argument: from the lesser (human fathers) to the greater (the heavenly Father). The argument establishes the disposition of the giver: the Father is more willing to give good gifts than any human father.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson notes that 'judge not' does not prohibit all discernment -- v.6 immediately requires the judgment of who the dogs and pigs are. The prohibition is against hypocritical judgment: judging others by a standard you exempt yourself from. The log-and-speck image is comic in its disproportion.

Robertson traces ask (aiteo), seek (zeteo), knock (krouo) as progressively intensive. All three are present imperatives -- keep asking, keep seeking, keep knocking. The father-son argument establishes the a fortiori: if evil human fathers give good gifts, how much more the heavenly Father.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Chrysostom: This is not the prohibition of discernment but the condemnation of self-exempting severity. The person who judges others harshly for sins they commit themselves is the target. Deal with your own sin first, and only then will you see clearly to help your brother.

Augustine: Enter through the narrow gate -- the road to life is characterised by difficulty and few travellers. This is not a statement about the absolute number of the saved but about the character of the way: narrow because it requires the death of self-will and submission to God's governance.

MacArthur: Do not judge, or you too will be judged (v.1). The prohibition is not against discernment but against the hypocritical judgment that applies different standards to self and others. The plank-and-speck image (vv.3-5) targets self-exemption from the standard applied to others. After plank removal, discernment becomes possible (v.5b).

MacArthur: Ask, seek, knock — the three imperatives intensify in effort from passive reception to active searching to persistent urgency. But the promise rests not on the intensity of the asking but on the character of the Father: if earthly fathers give good gifts, how much more will your Father in heaven give good gifts.

sections.header: Verses 6–14 — Pearls Before Pigs; Ask Seek Knock; the Narrow Gate

heb.paragraph: Three verbs in ascending intensity — ask (verbal request), seek (active searching), knock (persistent physical action). The present tense in Greek implies continuous action: keep asking, keep seeking, keep knocking. Prayer is not a single attempt but sustained engagement.

'Enter through the narrow gate.' The adjective stenos describes a tight passage requiring effort and deliberation to navigate. The wide gate requires no thought; the narrow gate demands intentional choice. The geography of the metaphor — gate, road, destination — maps the moral life as a journey.

hist.context: The Sermon's close is structured around three binary choices: two gates/roads (vv.13–14), two trees (vv.15–20), two foundations (vv.24–27). The middle unit (vv.21–23) is the hinge: the crisis is not choosing the wrong road but performing the right words without the underlying relationship. "Lord, Lord" with miraculous deeds but without knowing Jesus produces the most devastating verdict in the Sermon. The parable of the two builders then places the entire Sermon before the hearer as a decision: hear and do, or hear and not do. There is no third option.

refs.note: False prophets whitewash the wall — it falls. The two-builders image echoes Ezekiel's judgment on those who build on false foundations.
Lord, Lord, open the door — the five foolish virgins echo the "Lord, Lord" of 7:22. Both use identical language for those excluded at judgment.

notes.note: Calvin on 'Lord, Lord': Those who say Lord, Lord but do not do the Father's will are rejected because they never knew the Lord -- the relationship of trust and obedience was absent. The miraculous works were real but not evidence of genuine covenant knowledge of Christ.

Calvin on the two builders: The hearing of Christ's words is common to both -- many hear who are not saved. The distinguishing mark is the doing. The person who hears and does not do has not truly heard in any saving sense.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'wolves in sheep's clothing' discusses its OT background (Ezek 22:27) and application: false prophets do not announce themselves as false. They wear the prophetic garments. The fruit test is the only reliable evaluation because character cannot be concealed over time.

The NET note on the two builders discusses the contrast between petra (bedrock) and ammos (sand). The houses are identical in every visible respect -- only the foundation differs. The storm's violence tests what is invisible. The note identifies the eschatological dimension: the storm is the final judgment.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson notes false prophets in sheep's clothing identifies their deceptive method: they wear the wool of genuine prophets while being wolves. The fruit test is the reliable evaluation method -- not claims or charisma but the character their ministry produces over time.

Robertson identifies the two builders as identical in one respect: both hear the words. The difference is entirely in application. The house on rock hears and does; the house on sand hears and does not do. The identical storm tests both -- only the foundation's quality determines the outcome.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Chrysostom: You will know them by their fruits -- not by their words, reputation, miracles, or emotional appeal. The fruit that identifies the false prophet is the character their teaching produces: pride or humility, self-sufficiency or dependence on God, moral licentiousness or covenant faithfulness?

Augustine on the two builders: The sand is all human wisdom, achievement, and self-sufficiency. The rock is Christ himself -- obedience to him, trust in him, the life built on the foundation of his word. The storm is the final judgment that every life will face.

MacArthur: The Sermon closes with a four-fold set of paired contrasts: two gates, two trees, two confessions, two builders. Each pair is judged by outcome: the wide gate leads to destruction; the narrow gate leads to life. Jesus ends his greatest teaching with eschatological urgency — what you choose in response to this Sermon determines where you end.

MacArthur: The wise and foolish builders both hear the words and both build houses. The difference is not in hearing but in doing. The storm that reveals the difference is the crisis or judgment that exposes what the foundation actually is. Hearing without doing produces a structure that cannot survive the test.

sections.header: Verses 15–29 — False Prophets; Two Trees; Two Builders

heb.paragraph: They come 'in sheep's clothing' — externally indistinguishable from the flock. The danger is not bizarre teaching (easily spotted) but plausible teaching from apparently credible sources. Jesus says test them by 'fruit' — the long-term consequences of their influence.

The wise builder digs to bedrock (petra, not petros — the mass of rock, not a loose stone). The foolish builder builds on sand (ammos). The difference is invisible until the storm hits. The Sermon's conclusion insists that hearing without doing is sand-building — impressive but fatal.

hist.context: The opening of ch.7 transitions from the Sermon's teaching on piety (ch.6) to its teaching on community relationships and the conditions of genuine discipleship. The four units (judging, pearls/pigs, ask/seek/knock, golden rule) are united by the theme of discernment: knowing when to correct and when to hold back, knowing how to pray, knowing how to treat others as you would be treated.

refs.note: Love your neighbour as yourself — the golden rule (v.12) is its positive expression. "Do to others what you would have them do to you" activates love as the governing principle of all Torah.
All the Law and the Prophets hang on the double love command — the same claim made of the golden rule in 7:12. The Sermon and the controversy section both point to the same summary.

notes.note: Calvin: On 29 — False Prophets; Two Trees; Two Builders:  as Luke does, but with the human lineage — because his purpose is to show that the Christ who came in the flesh is the very one promised to David and Abraham. The order (David before Abraham) places the Davidic covenant foremost, since it is the more specific promise concerning the Messiah's nature and office.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: On 29 — False Prophets; Two Trees; Two Builders:  of cosmic or human origins. The note flags that Matthew may intend his Gospel to be read as a "new Genesis" — the story of a new creation beginning with Jesus. The translation choice "record" is more specific than the Greek warrants; "book of origins" would preserve the allusion to Genesis more clearly.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson: On 29 — False Prophets; Two Trees; Two Builders: tent of it. Robertson notes this is the exact phrase used in the LXX of Gen 2:4 (the heavens and the earth) and Gen 5:1 (the generations of Adam). Matthew's opening words place his work consciously in the tradition of the Hebrew creation and genealogical accounts.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Patristic commentary on 29 — False Prophets; Two Trees; Two Builders: omen, and it was not necessary to mention the maternal descent, since the whole matter of the dispensation was in the legal ancestry through Joseph. By showing that Joseph is of the line of David, he shows that Mary also is, since they would not have contracted a marriage between one of David's line and one who was not."

MacArthur: On 29 — False Prophets; Two Trees; Two Builders: tions." Matthew is announcing that his Gospel is a new Genesis, a new act of divine creation. This is not accidental: Matthew's Gospel is the most Jewish of the four, deliberately structured to present Jesus as the fulfilment of Israel's entire history and Scripture.

ppl.name: False prophets
ppl.text: Identifiable by fruit, not by claims. The tree metaphor is the Sermon's tool for discernment.

ppl.name: The crowds
ppl.text: Not the disciples but the wider audience. Their amazement frames the Sermon's ending.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Everyone who hears these words of mine and puts them into practice is like a wise man who built his house on the rock."NoteThe Sermon's final invitation: the parable of the two builders places the entire Sermon before the hearer as a binary decision. Hearing without doing is building on sand. The Sermon is not information — it is a foundation.

src.title: The scribes' teaching method
src.quote: Scribes taught by citing the tradition: "Rabbi Hillel says... Rabbi Shammai says." Jesus cites no authority except himself — "But I say to you." The contrast (v.29) is not between authoritative and unauthoritative teaching but between derived and intrinsic authority.
src.note: The contrast with scribal method is the Sermon's structural conclusion.

src.title: Palestinian house-building
src.quote: In Judean hill country, flash floods (wadis) could sweep through dry riverbeds after winter rains. Building on the rock outcrop above the wadi vs. on the sandy riverbed was a life-or-death decision every builder knew.
src.note: The parable is drawn from observable Palestinian building practice.

rec.text: The most-discussed verse in the Sermon for questions of assurance and judgment. Reformation theologians (Calvin, Luther) used it to distinguish between formal religion and genuine faith. The test is not extraordinary deeds but the will of the Father being done.

Jesus's teaching style, while distinctive, has parallels in prophetic literature where prophets spoke with divine authority. The uniqueness lies more in the scope and consistency of his authoritative claims rather than the absence of any parallels in Jewish tradition.

rows.label: Ask, Seek, Knock: Confident Prayer Based on the Father's Character
rows.text: Ask and it will be given; seek and you will find. The foundation of confident prayer is not the intensity of the asking but the character of the one asked: which of you would give your son a stone when he asks for bread?

rows.label: Ask, Seek, Knock: Confident Prayer Based on the Father's Character
rows.text: Ask and it will be given; seek and you will find. The foundation of confident prayer is not the intensity of the asking but the character of the one asked: which of you would give your son a stone when he asks for bread?

rows.label: Two Ways, Two Trees, Two Builders — 7:13-27
rows.text: Three parallel two-way structures close the Sermon on the Mount: narrow/wide gate, good/bad tree, rock/sand foundation. All three describe the same single decision: hear and do, or hear and not do.

tx.title: Do not give dogs what is sacred; do not throw your pearls to pigs
tx.content: The instruction requires discernment about who is capable of receiving sacred things.
tx.note: The verse prevents the 'judge not' command from being read as requiring indiscriminate sharing of sacred things. Discernment does not contradict non-judgment. Both are required.

debate.title: Matthew 7:21-23: Can Genuine Christians Be Rejected?

positions.name: These are counterfeit believers, never genuinely saved
positions.argument: The rejection 'I never knew you' (ouk egnon -- never at any point) indicates no genuine relationship ever existed.

positions.name: These are genuine believers who were unfaithful
positions.argument: The 'Lord, Lord' and works performed suggest genuine confession and service. The rejection is due to persistent unfaithfulness.

themes.note: Matthew 7 closes the Sermon on the Mount with the decisive application: the test of genuine discipleship is not hearing but doing. The two-way structures all point to the same conclusion -- the crisis of decision the entire Sermon has been building toward.

coaching.tip: 'Do not judge' (v. 1) doesn't prohibit all evaluation—verse 6 requires distinguishing 'dogs' and 'pigs.' The issue is hypocritical judgment: the log-eyed critiquing the speck-eyed. Self-examination precedes assessment.

'Everyone who hears these words of mine and puts them into practice is like a wise man' (v. 24). The Sermon ends with action: hearing without doing is foundation-less. Two builders, two storms, two outcomes.

prayer_prompt: As Jesus warns that the wise builder is the one who hears His words and puts them into practice, consider asking God to show you the gap between what you know and what you do.
